Q1: My study routine was honestly chaotic most of the time. I'm juggling 14 online courses with very limited resources, so I had to be strategic about what I focused on. I'd usually try to batch my studying into intense sessions when I could access good internet, often late at night when my anxiety was actually lower. I relied heavily on visual materials like diagrams and charts, so I'd spend time creating those from lecture content rather than following a traditional schedule.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I felt that week. With so many courses running simultaneously, I couldn't maintain regular times—I was constantly putting out fires. Some weeks I'd barely touch certain courses, then cram everything into a few marathon sessions. My anxiety would spike around deadlines, which ironically made me more productive but also more exhausted.
Q3: Usually panic about an upcoming deadline or a notification that something was due soon. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything critical because with 14 courses, it's easy to lose track. Occasionally I'd go in specifically to watch videos since that's how I learn best. But honestly, a lot of my logins were anxiety-driven rather than planned.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. With my low attendance and the sheer number of courses I'm managing, the LMS never became routine—it was more like a crisis management tool. The closest I got to routine was checking in Sunday nights to plan my week, but even that fell apart when life got hectic. I wish I could say it became habitual, but my engagement was always reactive.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me as a visual learner—I watched nearly 240 minutes of content because that's how information actually sticks in my brain. Discussion posts were also valuable because interacting with peers helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. I found that visual diagrams and any kind of flowchart or infographic made complex topics click for me in ways that reading never did.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and text-heavy resources because they just don't work for my learning style. I also delayed many of the smaller assignments, which is why my assignment completion rate is so low despite my exam scores being high. Sometimes I'd skip discussion readings if they seemed redundant to what I'd already learned from videos. Time was scarce, so I had to ruthlessly prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly prepared me for exams or helped me understand core concepts visually. If I could see how it connected to assessment criteria, I'd engage fully. Activities that felt like busywork or repetitive exercises got pushed aside because I simply didn't have the bandwidth. The CBT sessions I attended actually helped me evaluate this more clearly—identifying what genuinely mattered versus what was just anxiety-inducing noise.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones created pressure and anxiety, but at least they had clear stakes. With optional stuff, I'd feel guilty for not doing them but also relieved I could skip them without consequences. The irony is that some optional activities were probably more valuable than required ones, but my anxiety made me focus only on what was graded.
Q9: Absolutely, this was actually my primary study method. I'd download videos and PDFs, then study them offline on my phone during commutes or when internet was unreliable. I created extensive visual notes and mind maps by hand that never showed up in any system. I also used free YouTube tutorials and external resources to supplement course content, especially for topics where the provided materials weren't visual enough.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened offline with downloaded materials and hand-drawn diagrams spread across my desk. I'd spend hours converting lecture content into visual formats—flowcharts, color-coded notes, concept maps—none of which registered anywhere. Some of my best study sessions were in group chats with classmates, discussing problems and explaining concepts to each other outside the LMS entirely.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video once, download it or take detailed visual notes, and then review those materials multiple times without ever logging back in. My assignment scores don't reflect how much I actually engaged with the content because I was working from my own synthesized materials. This is probably why my exam scores are so much higher than my LMS engagement would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, especially mid-semester when all 14 courses seemed to demand attention simultaneously. My anxiety would peak, and I'd almost freeze—unable to decide what to prioritize. The CBT sessions helped me work through some of this, teaching me to break tasks down and challenge catastrophic thinking. But there were weeks where I just couldn't face the LMS at all.
Q13: Massively. My low resources meant I sometimes had to choose between buying materials or eating properly, which affected my concentration. Fatigue was constant because I was always trying to catch up. When workload peaked, I'd only use the LMS for absolute essentials—checking deadlines, submitting work at the last minute. Confusion about course requirements sometimes made me avoid the LMS entirely because it felt overwhelming.
Q14: Many times. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my brain would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about other deadlines or just too exhausted to absorb anything. Sometimes I'd watch videos but realize I'd retained nothing because my anxiety was too high. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. My anxiety made starting feel impossible—I'd worry so much about doing it perfectly that I'd avoid it entirely. The irony is that once I started, I usually did well, but that initial barrier was huge. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week, and suddenly it was overdue.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system, for better or worse. I'd barely touch the LMS until a deadline loomed, then I'd go into hyperfocus mode. This explains my sporadic login patterns—clusters of activity around due dates, then silence. It's not healthy, but with 14 courses, I couldn't engage consistently with everything, so deadlines became my triage system.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback boosted my mood and made me more likely to engage, but critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me avoid that course for a while. The CBT sessions helped me reframe feedback as useful rather than threatening. When I got good assignment feedback, I'd be more motivated to engage with similar tasks.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming given my situation. Having so many components and deadlines across 14 courses meant I couldn't follow any single course's intended structure. I had to create my own meta-structure across all courses, prioritizing based on weights and deadlines rather than pedagogical sequence. Courses with flexible deadlines were easier; rigid structures stressed me out.
Q19: Visual resources and video content made engagement much easier for me. Courses that relied heavily on text or had confusing navigation made things harder. Clear deadline visibility helped because I could plan across all my courses. What made things harder was when important information was buried in announcements or when the LMS layout wasn't intuitive—I'd miss things and then panic.
Q20: That's interesting because I'm almost the opposite—low activity but decent performance. But I can imagine someone clicking through everything anxiously, trying to feel prepared, without actually processing anything. Or maybe they're searching for specific information repeatedly. High anxiety can drive compulsive checking without productive engagement. Sometimes being in the system feels like doing something even when it isn't.
Q21: This is literally me. My exam score is 92.44 but my LMS engagement looks terrible on paper. I study extensively offline, create my own visual materials, and learn through methods that aren't tracked. The LMS doesn't capture the hours I spend with downloaded content or the peer discussions happening outside the platform. Performance and logged activity are very different things.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click, how long you stay. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm anxiously scrolling or deeply focused. They miss offline study, external resources, and collaborative learning outside the system. They also can't capture the emotional and psychological factors affecting engagement—my anxiety, my resilience, my actual understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just showing up. It's when I'm creating those visual diagrams, making connections between concepts, discussing ideas with peers. It's feeling curious rather than just anxious. True engagement for me is when I lose track of time because I'm genuinely interested, not just when I'm logged in and clicking through requirements.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my anxiety affects every interaction with the course, or how the CBT sessions helped me develop coping strategies. It wouldn't capture my extensive offline visual note-taking system or the peer support network I rely on. It can't show that my low engagement isn't apathy—it's strategic survival across 14 courses with limited resources. My high resilience and the effort behind my exam scores are completely invisible.
